title: Custom Roles & Permissions Architecture — Blockers and Prior Art
topic: Replacing Vector's hardcoded 3-role enum with a data-driven roles + permissions system that lets G_admin/P_admin create roles, assign permissions, and provision bespoke account types for external users. Maps current code/schema blockers and benchmarks the design against Jira, Atlassian Rally (Broadcom), and Azure DevOps.
summary: Vector's role system is hardcoded in 4 layers (Postgres ENUM, Go Role type, 21 frontend files, role-equality middleware); replacing it requires a data-driven roles+permissions+role_permissions schema, generalised role-ceiling on rank, and a creator-matrix table.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Key Findings
  3. 3. Current State of Vector's Role System
  4. 4. Blocker Map (DB / Backend / Frontend / Constraints)
  5. 5. Prior Art — Jira, Rally, Azure DevOps
  6. 6. RBAC Best Practices
  7. 7. Recommended Architecture
  8. 8. Creator Matrix — Who Can Make What
  9. 9. Bespoke Account Types for External Users
  10. 10. Trust-No-One Implications
  11. 11. Migration Strategy
  12. 12. Risks & Open Questions
  13. 13. Sources
  14. 14. Gaps
  15. 11. Story Tracker — PLA-0007

1. Overview

Vector currently models user authority as a closed, three-value Postgres ENUM (user, padmin, gadmin) with a hardcoded numeric rank ladder (10 / 20 / 30) used to enforce the role-ceiling rule. The product direction is to add a fourth role — Team Lead — and to make role definition itself a first-class user-facing capability: G_admins and P_admins should be able to create new roles, edit them, assign permission sets, and provision bespoke account types for external users (third-party agents, contractors, auditors).

This paper inventories every blocker that the current architecture imposes on that change, benchmarks how the three nearest competitors (Atlassian Jira, Broadcom Rally, Microsoft Azure DevOps) solve the same problem, and recommends a data-driven roles + permissions + junction-table schema that keeps Vector inside its Trust-No-One security posture. The companion plan PLA-0007 turns this into an A–W workflow with ADD/UPDATE/REMOVE badges; this paper supplies the evidence base.

2. Key Findings

  • The role enum is the single biggest blocker. CREATE TYPE user_role AS ENUM ('user', 'padmin', 'gadmin') in db/schema/001_init.sql is referenced by users.role, page_roles.role, the provision_on_first_gadmin trigger, and seed migrations that bake role names into UNNEST(ARRAY['user','padmin','gadmin']). Postgres can add values to an enum but not rename or remove them inside a transaction — making the enum the wrong shape entirely for a feature whose whole point is dynamic roles.
  • The Go layer hardcodes the rank ladder. backend/internal/models/models.go defines Role as a typed string with a Rank() switch. The role-ceiling sentinel (ErrRoleCeiling) and the RequireRole middleware compare via this static ladder — meaning every “can actor X act on target Y?” check is a compile-time string match, not a data lookup. 14+ RequireRole(models.RoleGAdmin) call sites in backend/cmd/server/main.go alone.
  • The frontend mirrors the closed set in 21+ files. app/contexts/AuthContext.tsx declares type Role = "user" | "padmin" | "gadmin" — a TypeScript union, not a runtime enum — and 21 components/pages do role === 'gadmin' equality checks. None of these would survive a server returning a fourth role string.
  • Bookmarks code enumerates roles by hand. backend/internal/nav/bookmarks.go:238 contains for _, r := range []models.Role{models.RoleUser, models.RolePAdmin, models.RoleGAdmin}. Anything that fans out per role must move to a table read.
  • Page registry is already data-driven for tags but not roles. db/schema/009_page_registry.sql stores page_roles(page_id, role user_role) — the join table exists, but its role column is the enum. Replacing the enum with a FK to a roles table is a one-shot migration that lights up dynamic page gating with very little Go-side work.
  • None of Jira / Rally / Azure DevOps ship bespoke external-account types as a primitive. All three approximate it via custom roles or custom security groups scoped to a project. Vector can do better by treating a “role” and an “account archetype” as the same entity (a permission bag) and tagging some as is_external = true for UI filtering and audit.
  • The role-ceiling rule must move from rank-on-enum to rank-on-row. Every existing call to actor.Role.Rank() < target.Role.Rank() becomes a join: load the actor's role row, the target's role row, compare roles.rank. The semantics are unchanged; the data source moves.
  • Creator matrix is a separate axis from rank. “G_admin can create G_admins, P_admins, Team Leads, Users; P_admin can create Team Leads and Users only” is not derivable from rank alone — it is a permission. We model it as a discrete permission users.create.<target_role> bound to a role via role_permissions, not as a rank inequality.
  • Pages tagged is_admin_menu become a permission, not a role check. Today, pages flagged as admin menu items are hidden from non-admins via role-equality. Once roles are data, the right primitive is permissions.menu.admin.view attached to whichever roles should see them.
  • The Trust-No-One model already gives us the right discipline. Tenant ID always comes from the verified session, never the payload. Role ID will follow the same rule: when an actor creates a user, the actor's subscription_id comes from auth.UserFromCtx and the target role must be one the actor's role has explicit users.create.<role_id> permission for — verified server-side against role_permissions, never trusted from the JSON body.

3. Current State of Vector's Role System

The enum and the ladder

-- db/schema/001_init.sql:14
CREATE TYPE user_role AS ENUM ('user', 'padmin', 'gadmin');

-- backend/internal/models/models.go:21
func (r Role) Rank() int {
    switch r {
    case RoleGAdmin: return 30
    case RolePAdmin: return 20
    case RoleUser:   return 10
    }
    return 0
}

Where the role is consumed

LayerTouch pointsMechanism
Schemausers.role, page_roles.rolePostgres ENUM column
Triggersprovision_on_first_gadmin (017)Hardcoded IF NEW.role = 'gadmin'
Seeds009 page_roles seedUNNEST(ARRAY['user','padmin','gadmin'])
Go — typesinternal/models/models.goTyped-string + Rank() switch
Go — middlewareinternal/auth/middleware.goRequireRole(roles ...models.Role) allowlist
Go — serviceinternal/users/service.go5 ErrRoleCeiling sites compare ranks
Go — routescmd/server/main.go14+ RequireRole(...) calls per route
Go — navinternal/nav/bookmarks.go:238Hand-written role slice
Frontend — typesapp/contexts/AuthContext.tsx:8TS union "user" | "padmin" | "gadmin"
Frontend — checks21 filesrole === 'gadmin' equality scattered
Constraint.claude/CLAUDE.md hard ruleHardcoded protected human accounts — orthogonal to roles, must remain

Already-data-driven scaffolding to extend

  • user_workspace_permissions (002) — per-user, per-workspace can_view/can_edit/can_admin ACL with granted_by. Already tenant-scoped, already RLS-friendly. This is the existing pattern to mirror at the role × permission level.
  • pages + page_tags (009) — the page registry is a table read, so discovering what surfaces exist is dynamic. Only the role gate on top of it is hardcoded.
  • Audit log (002) — already records actor + IP for user mutations. The new role/permission writes hook the same logger; no new audit primitive needed.

4. Blocker Map (DB / Backend / Frontend / Constraints)

4.1 Database blockers

#SiteWhy it blocksResolution shape
D1CREATE TYPE user_role AS ENUM (001)Closed set; cannot remove or rename values; foreign key target type can't change without drop+recreate cascadeNew roles table with stable UUID PK; users.role_id uuid REFERENCES roles(id); deprecate enum after migration
D2users.role user_role NOT NULLDirect enum dependencyAdd role_id uuid, backfill from current enum, swap reads to role_id, drop enum column
D3page_roles.role user_role (009)Same enum dependency, separate tableSame swap pattern; page_roles.role_id uuid REFERENCES roles(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
D4provision_on_first_gadmin trigger (017)Hardcodes NEW.role = 'gadmin'Compare against the seeded system role row by id (or by stable code column on roles)
D5Seed migration UNNEST(ARRAY['user','padmin','gadmin'])Bakes the closed setReplace with SELECT id FROM roles WHERE code IN (...) after seeding system roles
D6No permissions tablePermissions don't exist as data today — they are encoded as which middleware fires on which routeNew permissions(id, code, label, category) table; new role_permissions(role_id, permission_id) junction
D7No tenant scoping on rolesRoles must be tenant-scoped (a tenant's custom “Auditor” role must not leak)roles.subscription_id uuid NULL — NULL = system role (gadmin/padmin/user/team_lead), non-null = tenant-custom role; RLS predicate subscription_id IS NULL OR subscription_id = current_setting('app.subscription_id')::uuid
D8No rank column on a roles table (today rank is a Go switch)Role ceiling can't be computed from dataroles.rank int NOT NULL; system roles seeded at 10/20/25/30; tenant-custom roles allocate ranks within bands the system reserves

4.2 Backend (Go) blockers

#SiteWhy it blocksResolution shape
B1type Role string + Rank() in models/models.goCompile-time closed set; rank baked into codeReplace with type Role struct { ID uuid.UUID; Code string; Label string; Rank int; SubscriptionID *uuid.UUID; IsSystem bool } loaded from the roles table at request time (cached per-tenant)
B2RequireRole(roles ...models.Role) middlewareAllowlist of typed-string valuesReplace with RequirePermission(perm string) — route declares the permission code (roles.create, users.list) and middleware checks the actor's role has it via role_permissions
B314+ RequireRole(models.RoleGAdmin) route bindingsEach is a literal enum valueMechanical refactor to RequirePermission("..."); the permission catalogue is the single source of truth and lives in code and in the seeded permissions table
B45 sites in users/service.go using actor.Role.Rank()Rank read from typed stringRead from the actor's role row (already loaded by the auth middleware after B1)
B5nav/bookmarks.go:238 hand-written role sliceIterates the closed setReplace with SELECT id FROM roles WHERE subscription_id IS NULL OR subscription_id = $1
B6No role/permission CRUD serviceThe whole feature doesn't exist server-sideNew internal/roles service with List/Create/Update/Archive/AssignPermissions, gated by permissions roles.list, roles.create, etc., and constrained by role-ceiling: actor cannot edit a role whose rank ≥ their own
B7Sentinel errors only cover ceiling, not creator-matrixNeed a distinct error for “your role lacks users.create.<target_role>”Add ErrPermissionDenied("users.create.<role_code>")
B8Audit logger doesn't model role/permission eventsNew event types neededAdd role_created, role_updated, role_archived, permissions_granted, permissions_revoked — same audit table, new action values

4.3 Frontend blockers

#SiteWhy it blocksResolution shape
F1type Role = "user" | "padmin" | "gadmin" in AuthContext.tsxClosed TS unionReplace with type Role = { id: string; code: string; label: string; rank: number } and a useRoles() context hook backed by /api/roles
F221 files do role === 'gadmin' equalityString literal comparesReplace with permission checks: useHasPermission('roles.edit'); the auth context exposes the actor's permission set, refreshed on login and on role change
F3roleLabels: Record<Role, string> in 2 filesClosed-set lookupDrop the constant; render role.label from the role row
F4No role-management UIFeature doesn't existNew /admin/roles page (G_admin only) and /admin/roles/<id> detail with permission grid; uses existing Panel/Table addressables
F5No permission-set picker on user create/editCurrently only role dropdownReplace role dropdown with a role picker filtered by creator-matrix server-side; on edit, surface effective permissions read-only

4.4 Hard constraints (do not break)

  • Protected human accounts — gadmin@mmffdev.com, padmin@mmffdev.com, user@mmffdev.com: their role_id must point at the seeded system role with the matching code post-migration. The CLAUDE.md hard rule is unaffected by this work.
  • Trust-No-One — tenant comes from session, never payload. The same rule extends: target role must be verified server-side as one the actor's role can create. The frontend never decides creator-matrix; it only renders the server's allowlist.
  • Role ceiling — actor cannot act on or assign a role above their own rank. Generalises cleanly to data: compare actor.role.rank vs target.role.rank at every write.
  • Audit completeness — every role/permission mutation logs actor, IP, before/after.
  • Tenant isolation on custom roles — tenant A's “Auditor” role is invisible and unusable to tenant B.

5. Prior Art — Jira, Rally, Azure DevOps

5.1 Atlassian Jira — Permission Schemes

Jira's permission model has three layers stacked on top of each other:

  1. Global permissions — site-wide capabilities (administer Jira, browse users, share dashboards). Granted to groups, not roles.
  2. Project roles — named buckets (default: Administrators, Developers, Users) into which users and groups are placed per project. Roles are global definitions; membership is per-project.
  3. Permission schemes — reusable mappings of project-roles → permissions. A scheme is attached to a project, and a user gets a permission if their role membership in that project includes it under the attached scheme.

What Vector can take: the scheme abstraction. A “permission scheme” is a named, tenant-scoped, reusable role → permission map. Cloning “Default Scheme” is the canonical Jira workflow for tenants who want their own variant. Vector should ship system schemes (Standard, Read-Only, External-Agent) and let G_admins clone-and-edit.

Custom roles: Jira lets administrators create new project roles (custom) up to plan limits. Cloud Premium and Enterprise plans expose “custom roles”; lower tiers are stuck with Administrators/Developers/Users. The lesson is that custom roles are a power-user feature, expected on the higher SKUs — Vector treating them as universal is a positive differentiator.

5.2 Broadcom Rally (formerly CA Agile Central) — Workspace/Project Hierarchy

Rally has fewer roles than Jira but a more rigid hierarchy:

Permission levelWhat it can do
Subscription AdminTop of the tree. Can create/edit/delete workspaces, manage all users, set up SSO. Equivalent to Vector's G_admin.
Workspace AdminManages a single workspace and its projects. Cannot touch other workspaces. Equivalent to Vector's P_admin (with workspace scoping).
Project AdminEdits one project's data and members. Closest to a Team Lead.
EditorCreates and edits work items in a project.
ViewerRead-only.

Above that, Rally adds a Team Member checkbox — an orthogonal flag asserting the user is on the project team (separate from their permission level). This is interesting: Rally treats “is part of the team” as a property orthogonal to “what can you do”. Vector could mirror this with a user_workspace_permissions.is_member bool, but it adds complexity for marginal gain — we are not adopting it in v1.

Rally's downside is a fixed five-level ladder with no room for “a contractor who can edit but not assign” or “an auditor who can view across all workspaces”. Custom roles do not exist. Vector's data-driven design beats Rally on flexibility.

5.3 Microsoft Azure DevOps — Hierarchical Security Groups

Azure DevOps uses security groups at three scopes:

  1. Server / Organization scope — built-in groups: Project Collection Administrators, Project Collection Test Service Accounts, etc.
  2. Project scope — built-in: Project Administrators, Contributors, Readers, Build Administrators; plus user-defined custom groups.
  3. Object scope — per-repo, per-pipeline, per-area-path overrides.

Permissions are Allow / Deny / Not Set tri-state, with Deny winning. The Project-Scoped Users group is the model for external collaborators: organisation policy “Limit user visibility” restricts members of that group to see only the projects they're added to, not the whole organisation. This is exactly Vector's external-agent use case.

What Vector can take:

  • Custom security groups at project scope — equivalent to Vector's tenant-scoped custom roles.
  • The “limited visibility” pattern — a flag on a role that restricts which workspaces/portfolios its members can even see, regardless of explicit permissions. Maps onto a future roles.is_external bool + scoped read-allowlist.
  • Tri-state permission model — Allow / Deny / Inherit. Vector should not adopt this in v1. It compounds reasoning cost and is rarely used outside ADO. Stick with binary “has the permission row or doesn't”.

What Vector should not take from ADO: the depth of the inheritance tree (org → project → area-path → iteration → repo → folder). It is famously confusing and supports tooling complexity Vector does not have today.

5.4 Synthesis — what the three competitors agree on

PatternJiraRallyAzure DevOpsVector recommendation
System (built-in) roles existYesYes (5 fixed)YesAdopt — G/P/Team Lead/User as system roles, is_system = true
Custom roles per tenantYes (Premium+)NoYes (security groups)Adopt — on every tier
Permission bag attached to roleYes (via scheme)No (level is the bag)Yes (allow/deny per group)Adopt — role_permissions junction
Scope on roles (workspace/project)Yes (per-project)Yes (workspace/project)Yes (project/object)Defer — v1 is tenant-global; per-workspace overrides via existing user_workspace_permissions
External / limited-visibility flagPartial (anonymous user)NoYes (Project-Scoped Users)Adopt — roles.is_external bool for UI filter + future scoping
Tri-state allow/denyNoNoYesReject — binary is enough; one less foot-gun
Reusable schemes / templatesYesNoImplicit (clone group)Adopt later — v1 ships a built-in “clone role” action; named schemes can come in v2
Creator matrix (who creates whom)Implicit (admin perm)Implicit (admin level)Implicit (Manage Users perm)Adopt — explicitly: users.create.<target_role_id> as a permission row

6. RBAC Best Practices

The role-based access control literature converges on a small set of invariants that the Vector design should honour:

  • Least privilege. Default-deny. Permissions are added explicitly, never implied. The role User ships with the smallest possible bag.
  • Separation of duty. The actor who can create a role should not necessarily be the actor who can grant that role to a user. In v1 we collapse this (G_admin does both) for simplicity, but flag it as a v2 split for compliance-sensitive tenants.
  • Permission catalogue is closed and codified. Permissions are an enum-like list authored in code (permissions/catalogue.go) and seeded into the table on migrate. Tenants compose roles out of permissions; they do not invent new permissions. This keeps the test surface finite.
  • Permissions are dotted, hierarchical names. users.create, users.create.team_lead, roles.list, roles.edit, portfolio.archive. Names are stable identifiers; labels are localisable.
  • Multi-role assignment is optional but powerful. Most systems give a user one role; some give many and union the permissions. Vector v1 ships single-role-per-user (matching the current model) and adds multi-role only if a customer asks. This avoids the “why do I have permission X when I should not” debugging trap.
  • Role hierarchies via rank, not inheritance. True role inheritance (B inherits from A's permission set) is a feature with strong gravity — once added, every permission decision is “does this role or any ancestor have it?”. Rank-only is much easier to reason about: rank is used only for the ceiling rule, never for permission inheritance. Permissions belong solely to the role they're attached to.
  • Audit every grant and revoke. “User X added permission Y to role Z at time T from IP I.” Mandatory.
  • Cache aggressively, invalidate precisely. The actor's permission set is fetched once per session token and cached in-memory, invalidated when their role changes or the role's permissions change. Otherwise every request joins three tables.

7. Recommended Architecture

7.1 Schema sketch

-- New: roles table (replaces enum)
CREATE TABLE roles (
  id              uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  subscription_id uuid REFERENCES subscriptions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  -- subscription_id IS NULL  => system role (gadmin/padmin/team_lead/user)
  -- subscription_id NOT NULL => tenant-custom role
  code            text NOT NULL,           -- 'gadmin', 'padmin', 'team_lead', 'user', or tenant slug
  label           text NOT NULL,
  description     text,
  rank            int  NOT NULL,           -- 10 user, 20 team_lead, 25 padmin, 30 gadmin; tenant ranks 11..29 with system bands reserved
  is_system       bool NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
  is_external     bool NOT NULL DEFAULT false, -- external-agent / contractor archetype
  archived_at     timestamptz,
  created_at      timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at      timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE (subscription_id, code)
);

-- New: permissions catalogue (seeded from Go)
CREATE TABLE permissions (
  id          uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  code        text NOT NULL UNIQUE,        -- 'users.create.team_lead'
  label       text NOT NULL,
  category    text NOT NULL,               -- 'users', 'roles', 'portfolio', etc
  description text,
  created_at  timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

-- New: junction table (the actual RBAC binding)
CREATE TABLE role_permissions (
  role_id        uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES roles(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  permission_id  uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES permissions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  granted_by     uuid REFERENCES users(id),
  granted_at     timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  PRIMARY KEY (role_id, permission_id)
);

-- ALTER: users gets role_id, deprecates role enum
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN role_id uuid REFERENCES roles(id);
-- backfill: UPDATE users SET role_id = (SELECT id FROM roles WHERE code = users.role::text AND subscription_id IS NULL);
-- ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN role_id SET NOT NULL;
-- ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN role;  -- after all reads switched

-- ALTER: page_roles uses role_id
ALTER TABLE page_roles ADD COLUMN role_id uuid REFERENCES roles(id) ON DELETE CASCADE;
-- backfill, then drop old role column

7.2 Permission catalogue (initial set)

CategoryPermission codeGranted to (system roles)
usersusers.listgadmin, padmin, team_lead
usersusers.create.gadmingadmin
usersusers.create.padmingadmin
usersusers.create.team_leadgadmin, padmin
usersusers.create.usergadmin, padmin
usersusers.create.<custom_role_id>granted explicitly per custom role
usersusers.editgadmin, padmin (subject to role ceiling)
usersusers.archivegadmin, padmin (subject to role ceiling)
usersusers.reset_passwordgadmin, padmin (subject to role ceiling)
rolesroles.listgadmin, padmin (read-only for padmin)
rolesroles.creategadmin
rolesroles.editgadmin (subject to ceiling vs target role rank)
rolesroles.archivegadmin
rolesroles.assign_permissionsgadmin
portfolioportfolio.listall
portfolioportfolio.creategadmin, padmin, team_lead
portfolioportfolio.editgadmin, padmin, team_lead
portfolioportfolio.archivegadmin, padmin
menumenu.admin.viewgadmin, padmin (replaces is_admin_menu role check)
menumenu.dev.viewgadmin only

This is the initial bag. Adding a permission is a migration + a Go catalogue entry; revoking one needs a migration that drops the row from role_permissions as well.

7.3 Request-time check — the hot path

// internal/auth/middleware.go (post-refactor)
func RequirePermission(perm string) func(http.Handler) http.Handler {
    return func(next http.Handler) http.Handler {
        return http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
            actor := UserFromCtx(r.Context())  // already-loaded user with cached permission set
            if !actor.HasPermission(perm) {
                http.Error(w, "forbidden", http.StatusForbidden)
                return
            }
            next.ServeHTTP(w, r)
        })
    }
}

actor.HasPermission reads from an in-process cache keyed by (role_id, role_updated_at); cold cache load is one query joining role_permissions + permissions. The ceiling check stays separate: it's evaluated per write against the target row's role.rank.

7.4 Frontend — permission-driven UI

// app/contexts/AuthContext.tsx (post-refactor)
export type Role = {
  id: string;
  code: string;        // 'gadmin' for system; tenant-slug for custom
  label: string;
  rank: number;
  is_system: boolean;
  is_external: boolean;
};
export type Me = {
  user: User;
  role: Role;
  permissions: Set<string>;  // ['users.list', 'users.create.team_lead', ...]
};
function useHasPermission(code: string) {
  const { permissions } = useAuth();
  return permissions.has(code);
}
// usage:
{useHasPermission('roles.create') && <CreateRoleButton />}

The 21 files doing role === 'gadmin' are mechanically replaced with useHasPermission(...) calls. Where the underlying intent is “is this user the highest-rank role”, replace with role.rank >= 30. Where it's “can they see admin menu”, replace with useHasPermission('menu.admin.view').

8. Creator Matrix — Who Can Make What

Actor roleCan create →G_adminP_adminTeam LeadUserCustom roles (tenant)
G_admin (rank 30)✅ users.create.gadmin✅ users.create.padmin✅ users.create.team_lead✅ users.create.user✅ per users.create.<role_id>
P_admin (rank 25)❌❌✅ users.create.team_lead✅ users.create.user✅ if perm explicitly granted
Team Lead (rank 20)❌❌❌❌❌
User (rank 10)❌❌❌❌❌

Why ranks 10/20/25/30 not 10/20/30/40? Reserve gaps for future intermediate roles without renumbering. The user requested rank parity between “Team Lead” and “same rights as P_admin for now” — we honour that with permissions (Team Lead seeded with the same permission set as P_admin in v0) but separate ranks (25 vs 20) so the role-ceiling rule produces the right answer once Team Lead's permission set diverges. Rank is purely structural; permissions are the actual authority.

Server enforcement, not UI enforcement. The POST /users handler reads the actor's permissions, intersects with users.create.<target_role_id>, returns 403 if absent. The frontend pre-filters the role dropdown to only the roles the actor can create — but only as a UX courtesy, never as the authority.

Role ceiling on top. Even with users.create.gadmin, a P_admin (rank 25) cannot create a G_admin (rank 30) because actor.role.rank < target.role.rank. The ceiling fires before the permission check; both must pass. This is belt-and-braces — if a permission is mis-seeded, the rank check still saves the system.

9. Bespoke Account Types for External Users

The user's framing is that “a G_admin or P_admin may wish to create a certain account type that is dedicated to external users of the company, such as third-party agents” with permissions that differ from the main categories. The natural model is:

  • An account “type” is a role. No new entity. The role is tenant-scoped (subscription_id non-null), tagged is_external = true, with whatever permission bag the admin assembles.
  • The is_external flag drives UI affordances — appears under a separate “External” section in admin UI; user-list filtering; differently coloured chip in the user table; future audit-report grouping.
  • Default external permission set — create a system role external at rank 5 (below User) seeded with portfolio.list only and nothing else. Tenants clone-and-customise from there.
  • Login restriction (future) — roles.is_external = true roles can be required to authenticate via a separate IdP, expire faster, or have a session length cap. Not in v1; design accommodates it.
  • Time-bound provisioning (future) — external accounts often have a contract end date. Add users.access_expires_at as a v2 column; the auth middleware refuses sessions if past expiry. Not in v1.

Why not a separate “account_types” table? Two abstractions that each carry a permission bag is wasted modelling. A “type” in the user's prose maps cleanly onto a “role” in the schema if we accept that custom roles are first-class. This is the same conclusion Jira and Azure DevOps reached.

10. Trust-No-One Implications

The Trust-No-One posture (docs/c_security.md) extends to the role/permission system as follows:

  • Role ID never comes from the request body. When creating a user, the target role ID is in the body, but the actor's role and permissions are derived from the verified session. The handler resolves “can this actor create this target role?” entirely from server state.
  • Tenant ID always from session. Custom-role queries scope on subscription_id = actor.subscription_id. A G_admin in tenant A cannot see, edit, assign, or grant tenant B's custom roles — the SQL filter makes them invisible.
  • Permission catalogue is server-authoritative. The frontend may render any permission name; only the server decides if a permission grants a request. The catalogue lives in code (permissions/catalogue.go) and migrations seed it — tenants cannot invent permissions. This bounds the test surface and prevents permission-name forgery.
  • Role ceiling cannot be bypassed via permission. Even if a P_admin's role is mis-granted users.create.gadmin, the rank check (actor.role.rank >= target.role.rank) refuses the write. Both gates must pass.
  • Audit log is mandatory and immutable. Every roles.create, roles.edit, roles.assign_permissions, roles.archive, users.role_changed event records actor, IP, before/after. The audit log is append-only.
  • Permission cache invalidation. When a role's permissions change, all sessions for users in that role get their cached permission set invalidated — or at minimum the cache TTL is ≤ 5 minutes so the privilege-revocation window is bounded. Strict invalidation is preferred.
  • System roles are immutable from the API. roles.code IN ('gadmin','padmin','team_lead','user') with is_system = true cannot be edited or archived through the public API — the rank, code, and minimum permission set are fixed at the migration. Only their label and description may be edited (for localisation). Tenants compose around them, not over them.
  • Self-elevation is forbidden. An actor cannot grant their own role permissions they do not have, and cannot edit their own role at all. Enforced server-side regardless of permission set.
  • Hard rule preserved. The CLAUDE.md prohibition on touching gadmin@mmffdev.com, padmin@mmffdev.com, user@mmffdev.com credential fields is unchanged — their role_id simply points at the seeded system role row after migration. No password, email, or active-state mutation occurs as part of this work.

11. Migration Strategy

The migration is large but mechanical if done in the right order. The principle is add the new before removing the old at every layer.

  1. Migration A — create roles, permissions, role_permissions. Seed system roles (gadmin/padmin/team_lead/user/external) with stable UUIDs (gen_random_uuid() recorded in code constants for tests). Seed permission catalogue. Seed default role_permissions bindings.
  2. Migration B — add users.role_id uuid nullable. Backfill from users.role. Add NOT NULL after backfill verified. Keep users.role in place for rollback.
  3. Migration C — add page_roles.role_id uuid. Backfill, NOT NULL, keep old column.
  4. Backend B1–B6 refactor. Replace RequireRole with RequirePermission, swap rank reads to row-based. The codebase compiles and runs against both old and new columns simultaneously while we cutover.
  5. Frontend F1–F3 refactor. Auth context now returns a permission set; 21 files migrated to useHasPermission. Type union deleted from AuthContext.tsx.
  6. New routes & UI. /admin/roles list, /admin/roles/<id> detail, role-permission grid, user-create role picker filtered by creator-matrix.
  7. Trigger update. provision_on_first_gadmin compares against the seeded gadmin role row by code, not the enum literal.
  8. Bookmarks fan-out. Replace nav/bookmarks.go:238 hand-written slice with a SELECT id FROM roles WHERE ....
  9. Migration D — drop users.role. Drop the enum-typed columns. The enum type itself can remain or be dropped; dropping after all references are gone is cleanest.
  10. Test gauntlet. Existing tests must pass on the new data path. Add new tests for: tenant isolation of custom roles, role-ceiling on rank-from-row, creator-matrix denial, permission cache invalidation on role change, system-role immutability.
  11. Provision Team Lead account. The original ask — team_lead@mmffdev.com with password123! — is now a one-line insert: pick the seeded team_lead role, create the user, hash the password. This is the smallest leaf of the whole tree.

Each migration is independently reversible up to the point of dropping users.role; that single step is the burning of the bridge. We can ship steps 1–9 progressively without committing to step D.

12. Risks & Open Questions

  • Permission-cache invalidation bugs. If a session keeps a stale permission set after a role's permissions change, the user keeps revoked authority for ≤ cache TTL. Mitigation: short TTL (≤ 60 s) plus explicit invalidation broadcast on role change. Open: do we need NOTIFY/LISTEN-style realtime invalidation in v1, or is TTL acceptable?
  • System-role rank collisions. If a tenant creates a custom role at rank 25 (matching P_admin), what is the role-ceiling outcome between them? Decision: ranks within tenant-custom roles must be in the band [11, 24] or [26, 29], strictly avoiding system-role ranks. Enforced by a CHECK constraint and a Go validator.
  • Migration risk on users.role drop. Once dropped, rollback requires restoring from backup. Mitigation: keep the column for at least one production cycle past the cutover; only drop in a follow-up migration after monitoring confirms zero reads of the old column for two weeks.
  • 21-file frontend refactor blast radius. Risk of a missed equality check returning true for the wrong role. Mitigation: ESLint rule banning role === string-literal compares; CI fails if introduced.
  • Cross-tenant role leak. A bug in the SELECT that lists roles could expose tenant B's custom roles to tenant A. Mitigation: every roles query goes through a single repository function with tenant filter baked in; review-required PR.
  • Permission catalogue drift. Code declares a permission, migration seeds it, but a refactor renames the code constant without updating the migration. Mitigation: startup-time check that every code-declared permission exists in the table; refuse to start otherwise.
  • External-role login enforcement. The is_external flag is metadata in v1; nothing actually restricts those users. Open: do we need session-length cap, IdP separation, or geo-fencing in v1, or can these wait for a tenant to ask?
  • Multi-role per user. Excluded in v1. If a customer asks, the migration is non-trivial — users.role_id becomes a junction table user_roles. Flag as v2.
  • Performance of permission lookup at high QPS. The cache makes the steady-state cost ~zero, but cold-cache cost is one query joining three tables. At hundreds of fresh sessions per second, this matters. Mitigation: warm cache on session create; benchmark before launch.
  • Audit log volume. Per-grant rows on a tenant editing a 30-permission role is 30 audit rows. Acceptable for v1; consider compaction in v2.

14. Gaps

  • Jira's exact permission-scheme JSON shape was not fetched directly; structure inferred from public docs and community articles. Validate before mirroring any field name verbatim.
  • Rally's API surface for permission introspection (REST endpoints) not enumerated — the comparison is at the conceptual level only.
  • Azure DevOps tri-state Allow/Deny/Inherit interaction with hierarchical inheritance has corner cases (multi-group membership, deny-wins-vs-explicit-allow) that we are explicitly not adopting; the corner cases were not exhaustively mapped because they fall outside the recommended design.
  • Performance numbers for the recommended schema are estimates; benchmark before launch with realistic tenant data (e.g., 50 custom roles × 30 permissions each × 1,000 active sessions).
  • Multi-role-per-user impact on permission union semantics (does deny win? do permissions accrue?) is unspecified because v1 is single-role; full design when a customer asks.
  • External-IdP gating, session-length caps, and time-bound provisioning are flagged as v2 but their migration shape is not designed in this paper.
  • Whether the creator-matrix permissions (e.g. users.create.team_lead) should be auto-granted when a custom role is created (i.e. by default nobody can create users of role X until a G_admin grants the permission to a creator role) is a UX decision deferred to PLA-0007.

11. Story Tracker — PLA-0007

This section is the live build ledger for the data-driven RBAC work decomposed from this paper into PLA-0007. Two tables: the overarching acceptance criteria (each ticks off when its proof passes), and the 18 stories in Planka backlog (each ticks off when its card moves to complete). Claude updates these checkboxes as the build progresses.

11.1 Overarching Acceptance Criteria

Ten outcomes that, taken together, define “done” for the locking-mechanism contract. Each is proven by one or more stories below; security-critical ACs (immutability, self-elevation, cache invalidation, tenant isolation, protected-account preservation) are deliberately covered by both an implementation story and a dedicated test story so the locks cannot be bypassed silently.

11.3 How this tracker is maintained

  • When a story-card moves to complete on Planka, Claude flips its row in 11.2 to checked and updates status.
  • When all stories proving an acceptance criterion are complete AND its Proven by evidence has run green, Claude flips the AC row in 11.1 to checked.
  • The tracker is the single source of truth for build progress on PLA-0007; the corresponding fields on PLA-0007.json (acceptance_criteria[*].done, work_item_backlog[*].status) are updated in lock-step.
  • Locking-mechanism gates that must remain green throughout the build: tenant isolation (AC5), system-role immutability (AC2), self-elevation prohibition (AC2), permission-cache invalidation (AC3), catalogue parity (AC3), creator-matrix dual-gate (AC4), protected-account preservation (AC8). If any of these regress, the tracker reverts the relevant AC checkbox and a remediation story is appended.
